target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",
  "hasRawKey": true,
  "exactLookupFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw"
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

Observed result after fix: The current prompt is 512 chars, below the 520 char cap, includes `<omitted_skill_keys>`, preserves the raw advertised key as JSON data, and `formatSkillInfo()` resolves the first advertised key exactly as `skill-00<zero`raw`.

What was not tested: Live model submission was not needed for this prompt-rendering fix; the local proof covers the real prompt builder and CLI exact lookup formatter path.

Local script generated 80 workspace skills with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520`, injected the raw omitted lookup key `skill-00<zero`raw`, and rendered the same prompt path through `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()`. The after case also passes the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup.

Before (`79acf3289d`, pre budgeted lookup-key note):

```text

Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

After (`db926508c4`):

```text
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",
  "hasRawKey": true,
  "exactLookupFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw"
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: perf(auto-reply): move memory flush off the user-visible reply path
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** for a near-threshold auto-reply turn the orchestrator
  awaited the near-threshold memory flush (an embedded housekeeping
  LLM call) before starting the user-visible reply path, so every
  message that crossed the flush threshold paid the flush wall time
  on the **critical path** between user message arrival and reply
  delivery.
- **Requirement:** the user-visible reply should not wait for a flush
  whose only safety contract is "complete before the next compaction
  mutates session state."
- **Fix:** dispatch the memory flush from the reply orchestrator's
  `finally` block (after `replyOperation.complete()`), register the
  in-flight flush in a per-`sessionKey` map, and **preserve the
  pre-compaction safety contract** by gating the next turn's
  preflight compaction on a bounded barrier that awaits the prior
  pending flush.
- **Out of scope:** the flush body, the existing
  `memoryFlushCompactionCount` /
  `hasAlreadyFlushedForCurrentCompaction` dedup semantics, heartbeat
  / CLI / followup-runner paths, persisted session state format.

## Motivation

On a threshold-crossing turn the inline ordering was
`preflight compaction → runMemoryFlushIfNeeded (LLM call) →
runAgentTurnWithFallback`. The user-visible reply could not start
until the housekeeping LLM call returned. Moving the housekeeping
dispatch off the user-visible reply startup path lets the reply turn
begin as soon as preflight compaction has resolved, while still
resolving any required flush before a later compaction can mutate
session state.

## Files

- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` — dispatch the housekeeping
  flush from the orchestrator's `finally` block; gate the dispatch on
  the reply path being reached; register the in-flight flush with an
  abort callback.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts` — pending-flush
  registry, pre-compaction `awaitPendingMemoryFlush` barrier with a
  wait-cap and a bounded post-abort grace; post-barrier `sessionId`
  rebind; `logVerbose` forwarding for visible flush error payloads
  that have no caller-supplied surface.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts` — new
  `createMaintenanceReplyOperation` export. The post-reply dispatch
  passes a maintenance `ReplyOperation` (not the original, already
  `complete()`d reply op) into `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` so the
  embedded runner's `attachBackend(queueHandle)` call is not
  cancelled by the completed-operation contract in
  `reply-run-registry.ts:attachBackend`.

## Root Cause

- The deferred housekeeping LLM call was structurally on the
  user-visible reply critical path because it was awaited inline
  before the reply turn started. Its only consumer is the next
  compaction (the flush writes
  `memoryFlushAt`/`memoryFlushCompactionCount` so subsequent threshold
  evaluation is correct). The flush therefore does not need to
  complete before the reply begins; it only needs to complete before
  the next compaction begins.
- Forwarding the original `replyOperation` into
  `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` after `replyOperation.complete()` would
  cancel the embedded runner's `attachBackend(queueHandle)` call:
  `attachBackend` cancels any handle attached to a completed
  operation with reason `"superseded"`. The deferred dispatch
  therefore builds a flush-safe maintenance `ReplyOperation` per
  call; it is not registered in `replyRunState`, has its own
  `AbortController`, and only cancels attached backends when the
  maintenance op itself is completed or aborted. The original
  reply's `abortSignal` propagates one-way into the maintenance op
  so an explicit user cancellation still stops the flush.
- The barrier wait-cap (30 s default) is made **terminal**: on
  timeout the barrier invokes a registered abort callback that aborts
  the maintenance op via `abortForRestart()` (cancelling the attached
  backend handle and aborting the embedded run's `AbortSignal`) and
  then waits for the flush promise to settle. The post-abort wait is
  itself bounded by a 5 s default grace; if the flush still has not
  settled, the pending entry is removed from the registry and the
  barrier returns so subsequent turns are not blocked indefinitely.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

This PR is verified by two independent proof tracks. The first is a
**deterministic controlled runtime proof** that drives the production
`runReplyAgent` orchestrator end-to-end with controlled LLM-boundary
stubs to make wall-clock ordering and latency measurable. The second
is a **live Telegram + Gemini runtime smoke** that exercises the same
production orchestrator with the real network channel, the real
provider, and a real model call. The two tracks answer different
questions and are reported separately below.

### A. Deterministic controlled runtime proof (ordering + latency)

- **Behavior addressed:** for the near-threshold flush-required
  turn, the user-visible reply path no longer waits for the
  housekeeping LLM call to complete. The housekeeping run still
  runs, just after the reply has finalized. A later preflight
  compaction waits for any unfinished required flush before
  modifying session state.

- **Real environment tested:** the proof exercises the **production**
  `runReplyAgent` orchestrator end-to-end. Every orchestrator
  decision (preflight gating, post-reply dispatch site, pending-flush
  barrier, post-barrier `sessionId` rebind, maintenance
  `ReplyOperation` construction and abort wiring) runs unmodified.
  Controlled stubs are only in place at the LLM-boundary modules
  (`pi-embedded.js`, `model-fallback.js`) to inject **deterministic
  wall-clock latency** so the before/after delta is measurable:
  - housekeeping embedded LLM call: simulated 200 ms wall time
  - user-visible reply turn: simulated 50 ms wall time
  The stubs also mirror the real embedded runner by calling
  `params.replyOperation.attachBackend(handle)` (see
  `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:3378`) so the
  `createMaintenanceReplyOperation` contract is exercised on the
  real path. **This track does not use a live provider or live
  channel; the numbers below are deterministic controlled wall-clock
  timings, not a live-traffic benchmark.** Timestamps are recorded
  via a wrapped `ReplyOperation` whose `setPhase`/`complete` methods
  call `performance.now()`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Reproducible by anyone (these tests ship with the PR):

  ```
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts
  ```

  Supplemental 3-case timing harness used to record the before/after
  table below. The harness file is not committed; the invariants it
  asserts are already covered by the committed tests above.

  ```
  # BEFORE — checkout upstream/main copies of the three changed source files:
  git checkout upstream/main -- \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts
  # ...run the harness...
  # AFTER — restore PR HEAD copies and re-run the harness:
  git checkout HEAD -- \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  Terminal capture of the 3-case timing harness (invoked via
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...`). The harness drives the
  production `runReplyAgent` and records `performance.now()` at
  orchestrator phase boundaries:

  ```
  EVENT [    0.01 ms] orchestration.start
  EVENT [  710.44 ms] reply.phase.preflight_compacting
  EVENT [  718.94 ms] reply.phase.running         <-- reply starts here on PR HEAD
  EVENT [  816.17 ms] reply.agent_turn.invoked
  EVENT [  866.37 ms] reply.agent_turn.returning
  EVENT [  975.45 ms] reply.operation.complete    <-- reply complete here on PR HEAD
  EVENT [ 1022.77 ms] flush.embedded_run.started  <-- post-reply on PR HEAD
  EVENT [ 1223.52 ms] flush.embedded_run.completed
  ```

  Before vs after timing table (ms, controlled 200 ms flush + 50 ms
  reply turn).

  Reference: `t_orchestration_start = 0.00 ms` (entry into
  `runReplyAgent`).

  CASE 1 — flush-required near-threshold turn:

  | timestamp                    | BEFORE (upstream/main) | AFTER (PR HEAD) |
  |------------------------------|-----------------------:|----------------:|
  | t_reply_phase_running        |              1013.95 ms |       718.94 ms |
  | t_reply_complete             |              1221.74 ms |       975.45 ms |
  | t_flush_start                |               812.33 ms |      1022.77 ms |
  | t_flush_end                  |              1012.52 ms |      1223.52 ms |
  | **Δ pre-reply (reply-start)**|              1013.95 ms |       718.94 ms |
  | **Δ end-to-end (complete)**  |              1221.74 ms |       975.45 ms |

  Improvements on this case: **−295.01 ms** to reply start (Δ
  pre-reply) and **−246.29 ms** to reply completion (Δ end-to-end)
  with the controlled 200 ms flush latency. The flush still runs;
  it is just dispatched after the reply completes
  (`t_flush_start` moves from 812 ms (pre-reply, blocking) to
  1023 ms (post-reply, off the critical path)). Real flush wall
  time will scale the delta; this proof does not claim a specific
  production-time number.

  CASE 2 — no-flush-needed turn (non-regression):

  | timestamp                  | BEFORE (upstream/main) | AFTER (PR HEAD) |
  |----------------------------|-----------------------:|----------------:|
  | t_reply_phase_running      |                96.73 ms |        58.42 ms |
  | t_reply_complete           |               336.53 ms |       309.04 ms |
  | t_flush_start              |              (not fired) |     (not fired) |
  | **Δ pre-reply**            |                96.73 ms |        58.42 ms |
  | **Δ end-to-end**           |               336.53 ms |       309.04 ms |

  Flush not fired on either side (token total below threshold). No
  functional regression is observed in this controlled harness.

  CASE 3 — next-turn pending flush + preflight compaction
  (correctness):

  | timestamp                              | BEFORE (upstream/main) | AFTER (PR HEAD) |
  |----------------------------------------|-----------------------:|----------------:|
  | t_reply_phase_running                  |               347.07 ms |        55.79 ms |
  | t_reply_complete                       |               545.59 ms |       295.66 ms |
  | t_flush_start                          |               141.26 ms |       337.92 ms |
  | t_flush_end                            |               341.97 ms |       538.40 ms |
  | barrier_held_reply_turn_until_release  |            (no barrier) |            true |

  In the AFTER row the next turn's preflight compaction observation
  point `barrier_held_reply_turn_until_release = true` confirms that
  `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` awaited the externally-held
  pending flush before invoking `compactEmbeddedPiSession` — the
  pre-compaction safety contract is preserved.

- **Observed result after fix:** for the threshold-crossing turn,
  the user message reaches the user-visible reply path
  ~295 ms (≈29 %) sooner under the controlled 200 ms flush latency,
  and the reply completes ~246 ms (≈20 %) sooner; with no flush
  required there is no regression; with a pending flush from the
  previous turn the next compaction is correctly held until the
  flush settles.

### B. Live Telegram + Gemini runtime smoke (real-runtime validation)

Independent from the controlled-stub timing track above, the PR HEAD
runtime was also exercised against a real Telegram channel and a real
Google Gemini provider to confirm that the production orchestrator
path is reachable, dispatches a real model call, and delivers the
reply through the channel. This track is **not** a wall-clock
benchmark — it is real-runtime validation that the PR-modified
orchestrator handles real-channel traffic.

- **Real environment tested:** a local OpenClaw gateway running PR
  HEAD code with `runtime/current` symlinked at the PR worktree, a
  real Telegram bot endpoint (`@<bot-redacted>`), and the real
  `google` provider serving `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview`.
  `GEMINI_API_KEY` is sourced from a local `.env` file by the
  gateway's startup script; the gateway process env was verified to
  hold the key by name (no value read or printed).

- **No-compaction path (normal turn):**

  ```
  [gateway.log]
  <ts-redacted> [telegram] Inbound message telegram:<chat-id-redacted> -> <bot-redacted> (direct, <chars> chars)

  [<sid-redacted>.trajectory.jsonl]
  type=session.started   provider=google model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview source=runtime
  type=model.completed   provider=google model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview
                         usage={"input":24251,"output":212,"total":24463}
  type=session.ended
  ```

  Operator-observed Telegram delivery: a non-error assistant reply
  was received on the user's Telegram client and reconciled against
  the single `model.completed` event in the trajectory delta plus
  the session-store `totalTokens` advancing to 24,251 on the same
  turn. Compaction did not run (the session token total stayed well
  below the flush threshold).

- **Post-reply memory flush path (force-flush byte gate):**

  In a second live run on the same PR HEAD runtime, the session
  transcript byte size was inflated above the in-tree
  `forceFlushTranscriptBytes` (default 2,097,152) by appending
  non-conversation `custom`-type JSONL filler records. These filler
  records were validated through the production session reader
  (`readSessionMessagesAsync`) to confirm they are not extracted as
  user/assistant/tool messages and therefore do not enter the model
  prompt. Token counters were left unchanged (well below the token
  threshold). A single short Telegram message was then sent.

  ```
  [gateway.log]
  <ts-redacted> [telegram] Inbound message telegram:<chat-id-redacted> -> <bot-redacted> (direct, <chars> chars)

  [<sid-redacted>.trajectory.jsonl, post-inbound delta]
  # Run 1 — user-visible reply on the orchestrator's main path
  type=session.started   provider=google model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview source=runtime
  type=model.completed   provider=google model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview
                         usage={"input":20756,"output":65,"cacheRead":4104,"total":24925}
  type=session.ended

  # Run 2 — distinct runId, starts ~2.1 s after Run 1 ends:
  # post-reply memory flush dispatched from the orchestrator's finally block
  type=session.started   provider=google model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview source=runtime
  type=model.completed   provider=google model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview
                         usage={"input":8637,"output":525,"total":9162}
  type=session.ended
  ```

  In the same window, `sessions.json["agent:main:main"].compactionCount`
  stayed at `0` (preflight compaction did not run; the preflight
  byte gate is disabled in this runtime config, and the token gate
  remained below threshold). The Telegram client received the
  Run 1 reply normally.

- **What this live track shows:** the PR-modified orchestrator on
  PR HEAD dispatches a separate post-reply housekeeping model run
  (Run 2) after the user-visible reply (Run 1) completes, against a
  real provider on a real channel, with the run separation visible
  as two distinct `model.completed` events with distinct runIds in
  the trajectory and a ~2.1 s gap. The session-store
  `compactionCount` confirms that this dispatch came from the
  post-reply path, not from preflight compaction. All live session
  state mutated for the byte-gate run was backed up before mutation
  and restored byte-for-byte after capture (verified per-file with
  `cmp`); the inflated transcript was discarded.

- **What this live track does NOT claim:** a measured wall-clock
  improvement against a live model. Run 2's wrapper reported a
  non-fatal local-environment error during post-call processing
  after the model itself returned tokens; that bounded local
  outcome does not affect the dispatch evidence and is unrelated to
  the PR's code change. The deterministic controlled proof in
  subsection A remains the source of truth for ordering and
  latency. Cost / token-spend numbers are not claimed.

### What was not tested (across both tracks)

- A live wall-clock TTFT benchmark against a real model at scale.
  Live track B is real-runtime validation, not a performance
  measurement; controlled track A is the latency source of truth.
- Live behavior under sustained traffic, retries, or under-load
  conditions on the real channel.
- Token / cost effect.

### Proof artifact

- `docs/work-items/openclaw-memory-flush-post-reply/proof/gemini-telegram-real-runtime-proof.md`
  (captured against PR HEAD `793d43f99687e3a1047dd22b59cdffcea0857df8`)
  documents redacted log and trajectory excerpts for both live runs
  above, the backup / byte-for-byte restore steps used to keep the
  live session state unchanged after the test, and the schema
  validation of the filler record type used for the byte-gate run.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- The user-visible reply turn now starts before any near-threshold
  memory flush runs. Any required flush still runs after
  `replyOperation.complete()` and is awaited by the next turn's
  preflight compaction before any new compaction state mutation.
- **Removed user-visible behavior to call out:** previously, if the
  housekeeping embedded run surfaced a visible error payload (rare;
  e.g., the housekeeping agent reported a target-path write
  restriction), the orchestrator returned that payload **in place
  of** the reply for the same turn. With the housekeeping run moved
  off the pre-reply path, that same-turn substitution is no longer
  possible. When the flush surfaces a visible error payload and the
  orchestrator (post-reply dispatch) does not supply an
  `onVisibleErrorPayloads` callback, the flush body forwards each
  dropped payload's text through `logVerbose(...)` so it is
  recorded in the gateway log rather than silently dropped. If
  maintainers prefer to retain visible surfacing, it can be added
  as a follow-up PR that emits a deferred channel notification
  after the flush settles.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Diagram

```text
BEFORE (inline ordering on near-threshold turn):
  user message arrives
    -> preflight compaction
    -> ** memory flush embedded run (blocks the reply path) **
    -> reply turn (runAgentTurnWithFallback)
    -> reply delivered

AFTER (deferred ordering):
  user message arrives
    -> preflight compaction
         -> await any pending memory flush from the previous turn
            (bounded wait-cap + post-abort grace; abort + abandon
             on grace timeout so the next preflight never hangs)
    -> reply turn (runAgentTurnWithFallback)
    -> reply delivered
    -> replyOperation.complete()
    -> memory flush dispatched (fire-and-forget; maintenance
                                 ReplyOperation; registered in pending map)

  Next turn:
    -> preflight compaction
         -> await previous turn's pending flush (so compaction sees
            post-flush state)
    -> ...
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: unit + focused integration tests.
- Target tests added in this PR (all committed under
  `src/auto-reply/reply/`):
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > dispatches the post-reply memory
    flush after the user-visible reply path returns`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > dispatches the post-reply memory
    flush from the reply path's finally block even when the agent
    turn throws`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > does not block the start of the
    user-visible reply on a pending memory flush`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > passes a maintenance
    ReplyOperation (NOT the completed reply's operation) into the
    post-reply memory flush` — captures the
    `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` `replyOperation` argument, asserts it
    is a different instance whose `attachBackend(...)` stores the
    handle (does NOT cancel), and asserts the original reply's
    operation is in a terminal state and DOES cancel handles with
    `superseded`.
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > aborts the maintenance
    ReplyOperation when the original reply operation aborts
    upstream`
  - `runReplyAgent runtime config > registers the post-reply flush
    with an abort callback that aborts the maintenance
    ReplyOperation`
  - `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded pending-flush barrier > awaits a
    pending flush before invoking compaction on the next turn`
  - `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded pending-flush barrier > rebinds
    the active run sessionId after the barrier when the prior flush
    rotated it, even if no compaction runs this turn`
  - `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded pending-flush barrier > does not
    block heartbeat preflight on a pending flush`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush
    proceeds after timeout when the flush hangs`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush makes
    the timeout terminal: on timeout it invokes the abort callback
    and awaits flush settlement before returning`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > awaitPendingMemoryFlush
    abandons the pending entry when the abort callback fails to
    make a truly hung flush settle within the post-abort grace
    window`
  - `pending memory-flush registry > registers and clears a pending
    flush after it resolves` / `... even after it rejects`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > does not register the
    operation in the active-run registry, so it can coexist with
    the live reply operation for the same sessionKey`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > attachBackend stores the
    handle (does NOT cancel it) when the maintenance operation has
    not completed`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > attachBackend cancels the
    handle with 'superseded' if complete() ran first`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > propagates upstream abort
    into the maintenance operation's abortSignal`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > inherits the upstream's
    already-aborted state at construction time`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > complete() detaches the
    upstream abort listener so it does not survive past terminal
    state` / `fail() also detaches`
  - `createMaintenanceReplyOperation > complete() does not call
    clearReplyRunState (so it cannot disturb a live reply
    operation for the same sessionKey)`
  - `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded > completes the flush turn without
    throwing when an embedded run returns a visible error payload
    and no onVisibleErrorPayloads callback is supplied`

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: housekeeping run visible error payloads no longer route to
  the user-visible reply on the same turn.
  - Mitigation: text of each dropped payload is forwarded to
    `logVerbose`. Same-turn surfacing can be reintroduced as a
    deferred channel notification in a separate PR.
- Risk: a hung housekeeping run could keep the next preflight
  waiting on the barrier.
  - Mitigation: the barrier inside
    `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` has a wait-cap (30 s default).
    When the cap fires, the barrier invokes a registered abort
    callback that aborts the maintenance `ReplyOperation` via
    `abortForRestart()` (cancelling the attached backend handle and
    aborting the embedded run's `AbortSignal`), and then waits for
    the flush promise to settle. The post-abort wait is itself
    bounded by a 5 s default grace; if the flush still has not
    settled, the pending entry is removed from the registry and the
    barrier returns so subsequent turns are not blocked
    indefinitely. The abandoned flush may still complete later and
    mutate session state; that bounded risk is explicitly preferred
    over an unbounded availability hang.
- Risk: cross-turn ordering depends on the per-`sessionKey` map
  being correct.
  - Mitigation: the map is keyed by `sessionKey`, which is stable
    across the in-flush compaction (`incrementCompactionCount`
    mutates `sessionId` but not `sessionKey`).

target.title: fix(agents): strip plaintext model provider keys
target.body:
## Summary
- strip plaintext provider `apiKey` values after merge-mode preservation, source-managed enforcement, and provider compatibility transforms so old `models.json` keys cannot be reintroduced into the final prompt-facing catalog
- clean legacy catalog-only `models.json` plaintext provider keys before the empty-provider `skip` path can preserve them
- migrate existing `models.json`-only plaintext provider keys into the target agent's local `auth-profiles.json` before writing the stripped catalog when there is no configured provider key and no target-local auth profile for that provider
- keep inherited main-agent profiles and runtime/external overlays from suppressing local legacy `models.json` key migration for non-main agents
- migrate known/env-present legacy env-name catalog credentials such as `LITELLM_KEY` to env-backed auth-profile `keyRef`s, while preserving ambiguous env-shaped plaintext strings as inline local auth-profile keys
- preserve non-secret markers such as env SecretRef markers and custom-provider `secretref-managed` compatibility markers
- add regression coverage for generated plaintext stripping, merge-mode stripping, catalog-only cleanup, existing-key migration, fail-closed behavior, inherited-profile migration boundaries, env-present keyRef migration, ambiguous env-shaped key preservation, and pi model discovery compatibility

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated and legacy `models.json` files should not persist plaintext provider `apiKey` values. Cleanup should preserve upgrade auth outside the prompt-facing catalog, including non-main agent local migration boundaries and env-shaped legacy values that must only become `keyRef`s when an env marker or observed env value proves they are env-backed.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source from this PR branch at `b23d1d7c`, macOS, Node.js `v24.1.0`, `/usr/bin/python3` for fs-safe pinned writes. Provider keys in proof output are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
FS_SAFE_PYTHON_MODE=require FS_SAFE_PYTHON=/usr/bin/python3 node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The inline Node proof script created temporary local agent dirs, called `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, `discoverModels`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `createProviderAuthResolver`, and exercised these real paths: inline custom provider stripping, legacy configured `models.json` key migration, catalog-only cleanup, env-present `keyRef` migration, ambiguous env-shaped plaintext preservation, non-main inherited-profile migration boundary, and pi model discovery compatibility.

- Evidence after fix (copied live output, redacted):

```json
{
  "head": "b23d1d7c",
  "tempRoot": "<tmp>",
  "inlineCustomProvider": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "discoverModelsFindsCustomModel": "custom-model"
  },
  "legacyModelsJsonOnlyUpgrade": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsLegacyModel": "legacy-model"
  },
  "catalogOnlyLegacyCleanup": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsCatalogOnlyModel": "catalog-only-model"
  },
  "envPresentCatalogMigration": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": false,
      "keyRef": {
        "source": "env",
        "provider": "default",
        "id": "LITELLM_KEY"
      },
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "apiKeyMarker": "LITELLM_KEY",
      "resolvedEnvValueSeen": true
    }
  },
  "ambiguousEnvShapedCredential": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": true,
      "hasKeyRef": false,
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "preservedLocalKeySeen": true
    }
  },
  "inheritedProfileBoundary": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "localMigratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "resolvedWorkerKey": true
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the final `models.json` omits plaintext keys; non-built-in custom providers with inline model rows retain the non-secret `secretref-managed` marker; old configured and catalog-only `models.json` credentials migrate into `auth-profiles.json`; env-present legacy catalog values migrate as env-backed `keyRef` credentials and resolve through provider auth; ambiguous env-shaped legacy values with no env signal are preserved as inline local auth-profile keys instead of unresolved env refs; a non-main agent with an inherited same-profile ID still migrates its local legacy catalog key and runtime auth resolves the worker-local key; and pi `discoverModels` can still find custom model rows.
- What was not tested: I did not test a real paid third-party provider. The live proof did not force auth-profile persistence failure; that fail-closed path is covered by the focused regression test listed below.

## Tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.write-serialization.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.plan.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false -p tsconfig.core.json`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/models-config.ts src/agents/models-config.write-serialization.test.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/models-config.ts src/agents/models-config.write-serialization.test.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Refs #11829

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- start a local HTTP server serving test responses (HTML error, empty, valid script, escape attack). Step 2 -- extract the real `run_remote_bash` from the patched `install.sh` and call it against each endpoint:
```bash
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server**

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

**Sanitization detail**

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte):
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

**Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)**

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

**Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS**

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` and `validate_downloaded_script` functions reject HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. The HTTPS proof uses the real unmodified `download_file` function with `--proto '=https' --tlsv1.2` over TLS against a self-signed cert trusted via `CURL_CA_BUNDLE`. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).

target.title: feat(secrets): add Bitwarden/Vaultwarden secret provider
target.body:
## Summary - **Problem:** OpenClaw has no Bitwarden/Vaultwarden integration for secret management. Users who rely on Bitwarden (55M+ users) cannot store API keys and tokens in their vault. - **Why it matters:** Issues #58 and #24875 request this. Bitwarden is the most popular open-source password manager and is self-hostable via Vaultwarden — a natural fit for OpenClaw's self-hosted audience. - **What changed:** Added `BitwardenSecretProvider` implementing the `SecretProvider` interface from PR #16663, with 34 unit tests. Supports `${bw:item-name/field-name}` syntax. - **What did NOT change:** No changes to existing code. This PR adds two new files only. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Security hardening ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #58 - Closes #24875 - Depends on #16663 (SecretsProvider interface — must land first) - Related #17311 (env/keyring/1Password providers) - Related #7916 (encrypted API keys / secrets management) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes New secret reference syntax for Bitwarden: ```json5 { "secrets": { "providers": { "bw": { "serverUrl": "https://vaultwarden.example.com", "collectionId": "openclaw-secrets" } } }, "models": { "providers": { "anthropic": { "apiKey": "${bw:anthropic-key/password}" } } } } ``` Field extraction supports: `password` (default), `username`, `notes`, `uri`, `totp`, and custom fields by name. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — adds a new secrets provider that reads from Bitwarden CLI - New/changed network calls? `No` — delegates to `bw` CLI which handles all network communication - Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — spawns `bw` CLI via `execFile` (no shell) - Data access scope changed? `No` - Risk + mitigation: - `bw` CLI is invoked via `execFile` (not `exec`) — no shell injection possible - BW_SESSION and other credentials are passed via env to the child process, never written to disk or logged - `--nointeraction` and `--raw` flags prevent interactive prompts and ensure clean output parsing - All errors are mapped to `BitwardenCliError` with actionable hints, never exposing raw vault data ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (tested), Linux/Windows (mocked) - Runtime: Node.js 22+ - Requires: Bitwarden CLI (`bw`) installed and logged in ### Steps 1. Install Bitwarden CLI: `brew install bitwarden-cli` or `npm install -g @bitwarden/cli` 2. Log in: `bw login` then `export BW_SESSION=$(bw unlock --raw)` 3. Create a test item: `bw get template item | jq '.name="test-key" | .login.password="sk-test-123"' | bw encode | bw create item` 4. Configure OpenClaw: add `${bw:test-key/password}` as an API key reference 5. Verify resolution ### Expected - Secret resolves to `sk-test-123` - Cache prevents repeated CLI calls within TTL ### Actual - Works as described (verified via unit tests) ## Evidence - [x] 34 unit tests, all passing - [x] 0 lint errors (oxlint) - [x] 0 format issues (oxfmt) - [x] Tests cover: getSecret (14 tests), setSecret (3), listSecrets (3), testConnection (4), error handling (4), configuration (6) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: All 34 test cases covering field extraction, cache, error mapping, env isolation, auth modes - Edge cases checked: missing fields, null notes/URIs, locked vault, unauthenticated state, multiple item matches, CLI not installed - What I did **not** verify: Live Bitwarden vault (tests use mocked CLI responses). Live verification is possible once PR #16663 lands and the provider can be wired into `buildSecretProviders()`. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` — entirely opt-in, no existing behavior changes - Config/env changes? `Yes` — new `secrets.providers.bw` config section (only used if configured) - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert: Remove `bw` from `secrets.providers` config, or revert to plain-text API keys - Files/config to restore: `openclaw.json` (remove `secrets.providers.bw` section) - Known bad symptoms: `BitwardenCliError` with actionable hint messages for every failure mode ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: PR #16663 may not land or the `SecretProvider` interface may change - Mitigation: This PR depends on #16663 and documents the dependency clearly in code. If the interface changes, this provider will be updated to match. - Risk: `bw` CLI output format may change across versions - Mitigation: Tests mock CLI output based on current documented format. The JSON structure is stable and documented by Bitwarden. ## AI-Assisted - [x] This PR was AI-assisted (Claude) - [x] Fully tested (34 unit tests) - [x] I understand what the code does - [x] Verified against official Bitwarden CLI documentation (https://bitwarden.com/help/cli/) Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

Greptile Summary

Adds a Bitwarden/Vaultwarden secret provider (`BitwardenSecretProvider`) that implements the `SecretProvider` interface from PR #16663, enabling `${bw:item-name/field-name}` secret references. The provider uses `execFile` (no shell) for CLI invocation, isolates credentials via child process env overrides, and includes comprehensive error mapping with actionable hints. - **TOTP caching bug**: TOTP codes (which rotate every ~30 seconds) are cached with the general TTL (default 5 minutes), meaning stale/invalid codes will be returned after the first 30 seconds — causing silent authentication failures. - **`setSecret` notes duplication**: When creating a new item with `field === "notes"`, the value is stored in both the `notes` property and as a custom field named `"notes"` due to an incomplete exclusion in the ternary condition on the `fields` array. - **`setSecret` error swallowing**: The bare `catch` block when checking for existing items treats all errors (vault locked, CLI missing, network errors) as "item not found," masking the real error and attempting an item creation that will fail with a confusing secondary error. - Good security practices: `execFile` over `exec`, env isolation without mutating `process.env`, `--nointeraction` and `--raw` flags. - Thorough test suite with 34 tests, though missing coverage for the notes-on-create and TOTP caching edge cases.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- This PR has logic bugs that should be fixed before merging, but they won't break existing functionality since this is an additive, opt-in feature. - Score of 3 reflects two confirmed logic bugs (TOTP caching returning stale codes, notes field duplication on create) and one error-handling issue (bare catch swallowing CLI errors in setSecret). None of these affect existing code since this is a new, opt-in provider, but they will cause incorrect behavior for users who adopt it. The security model is sound (execFile, env isolation, no shell injection surface). - Pay close attention to `src/config/bitwarden-secret-provider.ts` — specifically the `getSecret` TOTP caching path (line 221-225) and the `setSecret` create-new-item branch (lines 238-269). Last reviewed commit: 35578de (2/5) Greptile learns from your feedback when you react with thumbs up/down!

target.title: Fix Codex native thread reuse for context-engine bootstraps
target.body:
Fixes #85975.

## Summary

- Preserve context-engine `thread_bootstrap` Codex native thread bindings through the startup token/byte transcript guard so large bootstrap turns do not force cold `thread/start` on every later turn.
- Keep native reuse bounded by the existing context-engine compatibility checks: engine/policy, projection epoch/fingerprint, dynamic tool fingerprint, and compaction-driven binding invalidation.
- Add regression coverage for token- and byte-oversized bootstrap transcripts resuming the warmed native thread while sending only the current prompt.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server startup binding rotation should not clear a still-compatible context-engine `thread_bootstrap` native thread just because the bootstrap rollout exceeded the native token or byte guard.
- Real environment tested: local patched OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-native-thread-reuse`, using Lexar scratch under `/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-codex-native-thread-proof-20260524-151049`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a live `node --import tsx --input-type=module` probe against the patched production `testing.rotateOversizedCodexAppServerStartupBinding` export. The probe wrote real Codex app-server binding, `sessions.json`, and rollout files for token-oversized and byte-oversized `thread_bootstrap` cases, then read the saved binding back from disk.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout:

```text
/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-native-thread-reuse
REAL_BEHAVIOR_PROOF case=token-over-86k returnedThreadId=thread-bootstrapped savedThreadId=thread-bootstrapped projection=thread_bootstrap
REAL_BEHAVIOR_PROOF case=byte-over-2k returnedThreadId=thread-bootstrapped savedThreadId=thread-bootstrapped projection=thread_bootstrap
REAL_BEHAVIOR_PROOF proofRoot=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-codex-native-thread-proof-20260524-151049
```

- Observed result after fix: both oversized native transcript cases returned and persisted the warmed `thread-bootstrapped` binding with `projection=thread_bootstrap`; the startup guard did not clear the binding.
- What was not tested: no live Discord channel or real Codex app-server network session was exercised from this PR branch; full broad validation is left to GitHub CI.

## Verification

- Behavior addressed: a saved `thread_bootstrap` binding whose native rollout reports 86k latest tokens now uses `thread/resume` and does not replay assembled bootstrap context into `turn/start`.
- Real environment tested: local Lexar OpenClaw worktree at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-native-thread-reuse`.
- Exact commands run after this patch:

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts --run
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run -t "starts a fresh Codex thread before resume when the native rollout is over budget|uses current rollout token usage before cumulative usage|clears native rollouts at the configured byte limit"
git diff --check
```

- Evidence after fix:
  - `run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`: 19/19 passed.
  - targeted `run-attempt.test.ts` native guard slice: 3 passed, 215 skipped.
  - formatting and whitespace checks passed.
- Pi/runtime risk review: parallel adversarial review found this change limited to Codex app-server startup binding rotation; Pi embedded-runner/shared compaction semantics are not changed.

## Notes

`thread_bootstrap` makes the native Codex thread the carrier of the assembled context until the binding changes. Startup transcript-size guards should not preemptively clear that binding, because doing so forces unnecessary rebootstrap and defeats the token-efficiency contract. Reuse remains gated by context-engine binding compatibility, projection epoch/fingerprint, tool fingerprints, and compaction-driven binding invalidation.

Full repository-wide suites were intentionally left to GitHub CI per the local-resource policy.

target.title: i18n(zh-CN): improve Chinese translations for Control UI
target.body: Improve Chinese (Simplified) translations across the Control UI, covering common UI strings, agent tabs, dreaming mode messages, cron job forms, and usage metrics. Replace ambiguous or overly technical translations with clearer alternatives. Key changes: - Replace "Dreaming" with "梦境" for all Dreaming-related strings - Improve jitter/stagger related translations for clarity - Fix untranslated strings in agent tabs, notes, and stats sections - Localize Tailscale serve references - Standardize zh-CN language display names - Update common section values for consistency ## Summary - Problem: Many Control UI strings remained in English or used awkward translations when viewed in zh-CN locale - Solution: Rewrote 30+ translations with clearer, more natural Chinese alternatives; added translations for previously untranslated strings - What changed: Updated `ui/src/i18n/locales/zh-CN.ts` - What did NOT change: All other locale files, non-locale source code, functionality or behavior ## Motivation Chinese-speaking users of the Control UI frequently encountered English strings or confusing translations (e.g. "Dreaming" left untranslated, "抖动" for stagger/jitter which has a different meaning in Chinese). This PR improves the overall zh-CN user experience by replacing ambiguous or overly technical translations with clearer alternatives more natural for native speakers. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR N/A ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Chinese (Simplified) locale had untranslated or awkward strings in the Control UI - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (2026.5.20) with zh-CN locale - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Modified zh-CN.ts with improved translations 2. Updated .i18n/zh-CN.tm.jsonl (translation memory) 3. Ran pnpm ui:i18n:sync to regenerate locale bundle 4. Loaded the updated gateway at server port 5. Set Control UI language to 简体中文 and hard-refreshed - **Evidence after fix:** - **Observed result after fix:** All targeted strings now display in improved Chinese (e.g., "梦境" instead of "Dreaming", "定时任务" instead of "Cron Jobs") - **What was not tested:** Visual rendering across all UI panels (spot-checked main sections) ## Root Cause N/A — not a bug fix. ## Regression Test Plan N/A — not a bug fix. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes zh-CN Control UI users will see: - "Dreaming" → "梦境" throughout dreaming section - "Cron Jobs" → "定时任务" in tabs, stats, and form fields - "Tailscale serve" → "Tailscale 服务" - "Browser enabled" → "浏览器已启用" - "Tool profile" → "工具配置" - Improved stagger/jitter terminology for clarity - Previously untranslated strings in restart confirmation dialog, empty job states, and job form now in Chinese ## Security Impact - New permissions/capabilities? **No** - Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No** - New/changed network calls? **No** - Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** - Data access scope changed? **No** ## Human Verification - Verified scenarios: Compared each changed string against the English source (en.ts) to ensure accuracy - Edge cases checked: Template interpolation patterns ({count}, {value}, {time}) preserved correctly; braces balanced; TypeScript structure intact - What I did **not** verify: Visual rendering (locale is compiled by Vite at build time) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? **Yes** - Config/env changes? **No** - Migration needed? **No** ## Risks and Mitigations None — locale data change only, no logic, no API surface, no new dependencies. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Chinese (Simplified) locale had untranslated or awkward strings in the Control UI - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (2026.5.20) with zh-CN locale selected in Control UI settings - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Modified zh-CN.ts with improved translations 2. Updated .i18n/zh-CN.tm.jsonl (translation memory) 3. Ran pnpm ui:i18n:sync to regenerate locale bundle 4. Loaded the updated gateway at http://127.0.0.1:18789 5. Set Control UI language to 简体中文 and hard-refreshed - **Evidence after fix:** - **Observed result after fix:** All targeted strings now display in improved Chinese (e.g., \梦境\ instead of \Dreaming\, \定时任务\ instead of \Cron
